subject: Chemistry
topic: 7.10 Reaction Quotient and Le Châtelier’s Principle

learning_objectives.statement: Explain the relationships between Q, K, and the direction in which a reversible reaction will proceed to reach equilibrium.

depth_profile: Students must understand that a disturbance to a system at equilibrium causes the reaction quotient (Q) to differ from the equilibrium constant (K), taking the system out of equilibrium. The system responds by bringing Q back into agreement with K to establish a new equilibrium state. Students must distinguish between stresses that change Q only (such as changes in concentration) and stresses that change K (such as a change in temperature). In either case, they must understand that the concentrations or partial pressures of species redistribute to bring Q and K back into equality.
assessment_notes: Students are assessed on their ability to calculate, estimate, or predict an unknown quantity from known quantities by selecting and following a logical computational pathway (e.g., performing dimensional analysis and attending to significant figures). They must be able to connect what is happening at the molecular level to a model for a system at equilibrium, specifically connecting the value of the equilibrium constant (K) and the reaction quotient (Q) to the dominant direction of the reaction. A common pitfall is using ineffective mathematical routines instead of connecting the value of the equilibrium constant to the experimental data or observations provided.

spec_checklist.can_do: I can explain that a disturbance to a system at equilibrium causes Q to differ from K, and that the system responds by bringing Q back into agreement with K.
I can identify that changes in concentration change Q only, while changes in temperature change K, and describe how species redistribute to equalize Q and K.
